title: The 'Naive' Assumption
concept: The model assumes all features (words) are completely independent of each other given the class, ignoring word order and grammar for simple probability math.

exercises.prompt: Why is the Naive Bayes algorithm called 'naive'?

options.text: It assumes all words are independent of each other.
It assumes that human language is always grammatically perfect.
It assumes that spam emails never contain real words.

exercises.explain: The word 'naive' in Naive Bayes comes from the simplified assumption that all features (words) are independent of one another.

cards.title: A Bold Assumption
cards.body: Why does one of the most famous AI algorithms have the word 'Naive' in its name? It is because it makes a massive, overly simple assumption about how we talk. It assumes that every word we write is completely unrelated to the words next to it!

cards.title: What is 'Naive' Assumption?
cards.body: The 'Naive' assumption means the AI assumes every single word in a message is completely independent of the others. It ignores grammar, sentence structure, and the order of words. It simply counts how often words appear to calculate its guess.

cards.title: The Bag of Words
cards.body: Imagine trying to guess a movie using a bag of random words without knowing the order. If you pull out 'wand', 'wizard', and 'school', you immediately think 'Harry Potter'. You don't need to know the exact sentence structure or grammar to make a great guess; just seeing the words themselves is enough!

cards.title: Spam Detection in Action
cards.body: In the spam message 'Winner click here!', a Naive Bayes classifier calculates the probability of 'Winner', 'click', and 'here' happening in spam. It treats 'Winner' and 'click' as having zero connection to each other, multiplying their separate probabilities together.

cards.title: Simple but Powerful
cards.body: You might think that ignoring grammar makes an AI terrible at understanding text. Surprisingly, even though this 'naive' assumption is technically wrong, the model still works incredibly well and fast for tasks like sorting spam emails or detecting happy vs. sad reviews!

cards.title: Connected vs. Independent
cards.body: Standard language models look at how words connect. Naive Bayes looks at words as isolated islands, which makes the math incredibly fast and easy.

right.text: Sees 'ice' and 'cream' as two totally independent words.

left.text: Sees 'ice' and 'cream' as a single linked concept.

exercises.prompt: What does a Naive Bayes model completely ignore when analyzing a sentence?

options.text: The total count of the words

exercises.explain: Naive Bayes completely ignores the order of words and how they connect grammatically. It just treats them as individual, isolated clues.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Because the 'naive' assumption is technically incorrect, Naive Bayes is rarely used in real-world applications.
exercises.explain: Even though the naive assumption is a major simplification, Naive Bayes is highly effective and widely used in real-world spam filters.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to describe how Naive Bayes treats words in a sentence.
exercises.sentence: The model assumes all words are completely [blank] of each other.
exercises.explain: Naive Bayes assumes all features (words) are independent of each other, meaning the presence of one word doesn't affect the probability of another.

exercises.prompt: Which AI correctly explains why the model's assumption is called 'naive'?

options.text: The model uses advanced grammar rules to understand the exact context of each sentence.
The model assumes every word is independent, ignoring how words group together or change each other's meaning.
The model is called 'naive' because it only works on very short sentences.

exercises.explain: The 'Smart AI' correctly identifies that the Naive Bayes model ignores word order and treats words as independent clues, which is why it is called 'naive'.

exercises.prompt: If a customer reviews a game saying "not good", how does a Naive Bayes model handle these two words under its "naive" assumption?

options.text: It tries to fix the grammar before analyzing.
It treats 'not' and 'good' as separate, independent clues.
It refuses to classify sentences with negative words.
It automatically groups them into the phrase 'not good'.

exercises.explain: Naive Bayes treats every word as independent. Even though 'not' completely flips the meaning of 'good' when they are next to each other, the 'naive' assumption looks at them as completely separate clues.
